0.summary: The initial position. White opens 1.e4, heading for the Scotch after 1...e5 2.Nf3 Nc6 3.d4 — and has a prepared answer to every other defence.
0.why: Select a move in the tree to see its rationale.
0.logic: The plan: 1.e4, Nf3, d4 — open the centre fast, develop with threats, and centralise. Sidelines are met with simple, structurally sound solutions (2.c3 vs the Sicilian, the Advance vs the Caro-Kann, the exchange vs the French).
plans: Play 1.e4 and meet 1...e5 with Nf3 and the d4 strike (the Scotch).
Against the Sicilian: 2.c3 and rebuild the full pawn centre.
Against everything else: take the centre with d4 and develop naturally.
0.hook: e4 → Nf3 → d4: open it up.

1.summary: Material is level. Central pawns: White pawn on e4. Development: White has 0 minor piece(s) out, Black 0. Kings — White is still in the centre (can still castle); Black is still in the centre (can still castle). Pawn structure is still healthy and symmetrical-ish. It is Black's move.
1.why: 1.e4 advances a pawn to e4. Stakes a claim in the centre and frees the queen and king's bishop in one move — the classical way to fight for the initiative from move one.
1.logic: The repertoire's identity: open games, fast development, and an early central strike. 1.e4 aims for positions where knowing *why* beats memorising *what*.
1.prevents: Takes d5 and f5 under control before Black can claim them, and denies Black any easy symmetric equaliser except entering White's preparation.
1.tactics: No immediate forcing tactics: no checks, no winning captures and no loose pieces. The position is calm and about plans, not tricks.
plans: Develop knights before bishops — Nf3 hitting e5 comes first.
Strike in the centre with d4 as early as possible (the Scotch idea).
Castle quickly: short in the quiet lines, long in the Qxd4 lines.
1.hook: Open games on purpose: centre, development, initiative.
1.mistake: Don't drift into 'hope chess' after 1.e4 — the repertoire's edge comes from knowing the plan against each defence, not from the first move itself.

2.summary: Material is level. Central pawns: White pawn on e4, Black pawn on e5. Development: White has 0 minor piece(s) out, Black 0. Kings — White is still in the centre (can still castle); Black is still in the centre (can still castle). Pawn structure is still healthy and symmetrical-ish. It is White's move.
2.why: 1...e5 advances a pawn to e5. The classical reply: Black stakes an equal claim in the centre. Both sides now fight for the initiative in an open game — exactly where the Scotch strike works best.
2.logic: Exactly what the repertoire hopes for — the Scotch strike is prepared and White knows these structures better. Against e5, the repertoire continues 2.Nf3.
2.prevents: More a constructive move than a preventive one: it denies the opponent easy targets and keeps the position free of weaknesses.

alts.idea: The Scandinavian: Black challenges e4 at once, accepting an early queen excursion after the capture on d5 — time White will collect with developing moves. The repertoire answers 2.exd5.
alts.why_not_preferred: Covered by the repertoire as a side line; the main move e5 is the primary recommendation.

alts.idea: The French: solid but a little passive. The repertoire answers 2.d4.
alts.why_not_preferred: Covered by the repertoire as a side line; the main move e5 is the primary recommendation.

alts.idea: The Sicilian: Black fights for d4 with a wing pawn and aims for unbalanced play. The repertoire answers 2.c3.
alts.why_not_preferred: Covered by the repertoire as a side line; the main move e5 is the primary recommendation.

alts.idea: The Caro-Kann: like the French but with the light-squared bishop's diagonal kept open. The repertoire answers 2.d4.
alts.why_not_preferred: Covered by the repertoire as a side line; the main move e5 is the primary recommendation.

2.tactics: No immediate forcing tactics: no checks, no winning captures and no loose pieces. The position is calm and about plans, not tricks.
plans: Finish development quickly — every piece toward the centre.
Keep or increase the central superiority; meet wing play with central play.
Choose the right castling: short in quiet lines, long in the Qxd4 setup.
2.hook: Remember 1...e5 as a natural building move in the Scotch Game setup.
2.mistake: Don't drift: keep developing and castle before starting an attack, and don't push pawns in front of your own king without a reason.

3.summary: Material is level. Central pawns: White pawn on e4, Black pawn on e5. Development: White has 1 minor piece(s) out, Black 0. Kings — White is still in the centre (can still castle); Black is still in the centre (can still castle). Pawn structure is still healthy and symmetrical-ish. Loose/attacked pieces — Black: pawn on e5. It is Black's move.
3.why: 2.Nf3 develops/repositions the knight to f3. Develops with a threat (the e5-pawn). Every developing move that also creates a threat gains time — the opponent must react instead of developing.
3.logic: Step two of the plan: develop with a threat, provoke a committal defence of e5, and only then strike with d4.
3.prevents: Stops liberating ideas based on ...d5 or holding e5 for free — Black must commit a defender first.
3.tactics: Loose Black pieces: pawn on e5 (undefended).
plans: Play d4 next and open the centre while Black is committed to e5.
Meet 2...Nf6 (Petroff) with the critical 3.Nxe5.
Against 2...d6, exchange in the centre and aim for the queen-trade endgame.
3.hook: Develop with a threat — make them defend, not develop.
3.mistake: Don't drift: keep developing and castle before starting an attack, and don't push pawns in front of your own king without a reason.

4.summary: Material is level. Central pawns: White pawn on e4, Black pawn on e5. Development: White has 1 minor piece(s) out, Black 1. Kings — White is still in the centre (can still castle); Black is still in the centre (can still castle). Pawn structure is still healthy and symmetrical-ish. It is White's move.
4.why: 2...Nc6 develops/repositions the knight to c6. The main move: develop and defend e5. Now comes the repertoire's signature strike, 3.d4.
4.logic: This move follows the repertoire's guiding ideas: open the centre early (d4!), develop with threats, centralise — even the queen, when she cannot be harassed — and steer for structures Black must defend precisely: queen-trade endgames, doubled pawns, kings stuck in the centre. Against Nc6, the repertoire continues 3.d4.
4.prevents: More a constructive move than a preventive one: it denies the opponent easy targets and keeps the position free of weaknesses.

alts.idea: The Petroff: instead of defending e5, Black counter-attacks e4. The repertoire answers 3.Nxe5.
alts.why_not_preferred: Covered by the repertoire as a side line; the main move Nc6 is the primary recommendation.

alts.idea: Philidor-style: ...d6 defends e5 but blocks the f8-bishop. The repertoire answers 3.d4.
alts.why_not_preferred: Covered by the repertoire as a side line; the main move Nc6 is the primary recommendation.

alts.idea: Actively posted on the a7–g1 diagonal, eyeing d4 and f2 — the most natural square for the bishop, and the repertoire meets it with concrete play in the centre. The repertoire answers 3.Bc4.
alts.why_not_preferred: Covered by the repertoire as a side line; the main move Nc6 is the primary recommendation.

alts.idea: Defending the pawn with 2...f6? is Damiano's old mistake: the f-pawn is the worst defender because it opens the e8–h5 diagonal toward the king. The repertoire answers 3.Bc4.
alts.why_not_preferred: Covered by the repertoire as a side line; the main move Nc6 is the primary recommendation.

4.tactics: No immediate forcing tactics: no checks, no winning captures and no loose pieces. The position is calm and about plans, not tricks.
plans: Finish development quickly — every piece toward the centre.
Keep or increase the central superiority; meet wing play with central play.
Choose the right castling: short in quiet lines, long in the Qxd4 setup.
4.hook: Remember 2...Nc6 as a natural building move in the Scotch Game setup.
4.mistake: Don't drift: keep developing and castle before starting an attack, and don't push pawns in front of your own king without a reason.

5.summary: Material is level. Central pawns: White pawn on d4, White pawn on e4, Black pawn on e5. Development: White has 1 minor piece(s) out, Black 1. Kings — White is still in the centre (can still castle); Black is still in the centre (can still castle). Pawn structure is still healthy and symmetrical-ish. It is Black's move.
5.why: 3.d4 advances a pawn to d4. The Scotch strike: open the centre immediately, before Black has finished developing. After ...exd4 White recaptures with a piece that lands actively in the centre.
5.logic: This is the Scotch. Instead of slow Italian manoeuvring, White forces matters in the centre on move three, taking the initiative while Black's pieces are still at home.
5.prevents: Denies Black the comfortable, slow development of the Italian/Ruy structures — the centre opens before Black's pieces are ready for it.
plans: Recapture on d4 with the knight — it belongs in the centre.
If knights come off on d4, retake with the queen and build Nc3/Bg5/O-O-O.
Develop fast and keep Black reacting; the initiative is the point.
5.hook: Scotch = strike with d4 before they're ready.
5.mistake: Don't prepare d4 slowly with c3 here (that's a different opening) — the strike works *because* it comes before Black is developed.

6.summary: Material favours Black by 1 point(s). Central pawns: Black pawn on d4, White pawn on e4. Development: White has 1 minor piece(s) out, Black 1. Kings — White is still in the centre (can still castle); Black is still in the centre (can still castle). Pawn structure: Black has doubled pawns on the d-file. It is White's move.
6.why: 3...exd4 the pawn captures the pawn on d4. Black releases the central tension — the main line. White recaptures with a piece and keeps lasting central superiority.
6.logic: This move follows the repertoire's guiding ideas: open the centre early (d4!), develop with threats, centralise — even the queen, when she cannot be harassed — and steer for structures Black must defend precisely: queen-trade endgames, doubled pawns, kings stuck in the centre. Against exd4, the repertoire continues 4.Nxd4.
6.prevents: More a constructive move than a preventive one: it denies the opponent easy targets and keeps the position free of weaknesses.

alts.idea: Solid: it shores up the centre and frees the c8-bishop, at the cost of a passive bishop on f8 and no immediate counterplay. The repertoire answers 4.dxe5.
alts.why_not_preferred: Covered by the repertoire as a side line; the main move exd4 is the primary recommendation.

alts.idea: Develops and hits e4 — the most natural move on the board. The repertoire answers 4.d5.
alts.why_not_preferred: Covered by the repertoire as a side line; the main move exd4 is the primary recommendation.

alts.idea: Propping up the centre with the f-pawn — structurally the worst defender: it weakens the king's diagonal and develops nothing. The repertoire answers 4.Bc4.
alts.why_not_preferred: Covered by the repertoire as a side line; the main move exd4 is the primary recommendation.

alts.idea: Another move available to Black in this position. The repertoire answers 4.exd5.
alts.why_not_preferred: Covered by the repertoire as a side line; the main move exd4 is the primary recommendation.

6.tactics: No immediate forcing tactics: no checks, no winning captures and no loose pieces. The position is calm and about plans, not tricks.
plans: Finish development quickly — every piece toward the centre.
Keep or increase the central superiority; meet wing play with central play.
Choose the right castling: short in quiet lines, long in the Qxd4 setup.
6.hook: They released the tension — the centre is yours now.
6.mistake: Don't drift: keep developing and castle before starting an attack, and don't push pawns in front of your own king without a reason.

7.summary: Material is level. Central pawns: White pawn on e4. Development: White has 1 minor piece(s) out, Black 1. Kings — White is still in the centre (can still castle); Black is still in the centre (can still castle). Pawn structure is still healthy and symmetrical-ish. It is Black's move.
7.why: 4.Nxd4 the knight captures the pawn on d4. Recaptures toward the centre: the knight is superbly placed on d4, and White enjoys freer development and open lines.
7.logic: The Scotch tabiya: a centralised knight, open c1- and d1-diagonals... every White piece gets a good square out of this structure.
7.prevents: More a constructive move than a preventive one: it denies the opponent easy targets and keeps the position free of weaknesses.
plans: Finish development quickly — every piece toward the centre.
Keep or increase the central superiority; meet wing play with central play.
Choose the right castling: short in quiet lines, long in the Qxd4 setup.
7.mistake: Don't drift: keep developing and castle before starting an attack, and don't push pawns in front of your own king without a reason.

8.summary: Material favours Black by 3 point(s). Central pawns: White pawn on e4. Development: White has 0 minor piece(s) out, Black 1. 9.summary: Material is level. Central pawns: White pawn on e4. Development: White has 0 minor piece(s) out, Black 0. Kings — White is still in the centre (can still castle); Black is still in the centre (can still castle). Pawn structure is still healthy and symmetrical-ish. It is Black's move.
9.why: 5.Qxd4 the queen captures the knight on d4. Normally the queen shouldn't come out early — but the c6-knight that would harass her has just been traded. She sits on d4 like a monarch: central, safe, and controlling both wings. This is the repertoire's core idea.
9.logic: The repertoire's pet line. After the knight trade the classic punishment for an early queen (a tempo-gaining ...Nc6) no longer exists, so White gets a dream setup for free: queen on d4, Nc3, Bg5 and O-O-O.
9.prevents: The immediate harassers are gone — that's the point. (...c5 or ...Bc5 can still nudge the queen later, which is exactly why the calm Qd1 retreat is part of the repertoire.)
plans: Develop Nc3 and Bg5 around the queen — she's a strongpoint, not a target.
Castle long: the rook lands on d1 with tempo against d6/d7.
If ...c5 arrives, retreat Qd1 calmly — the lead in development remains.
9.hook: Qxd4 is safe here — the harasser (that c6-knight) is gone.
9.mistake: Two opposite errors: avoiding 5.Qxd4 because 'the queen shouldn't come out early' (the rule's reason — tempo-gaining attacks — is absent here), and leaving her centralised once ...c5/...Bc5 arrive with real tempo. Centralise free, retreat taxed.

13.summary: Material is level. Central pawns: White pawn on e4. Development: White has 2 minor piece(s) out, Black 1. Kings — White is still in the centre (can still castle); Black is still in the centre (can still castle). Pawn structure is still healthy and symmetrical-ish. It is Black's move.
13.why: 7.Bg5 develops/repositions the bishop to g5. Develops with pressure on the f6-knight. The pin kills ...Nxe4-style tricks and softens Black's kingside dark squares.
13.logic: The third piece of the Qxd4 machine: the pin ties Black's best defender to the board while White finishes the plan with O-O-O.
13.prevents: Freezes the f6-knight: no ...Nxe4 tricks, and any ...h6/...g5 attempt to break the pin weakens Black's own king.
13.tactics: No immediate forcing tactics: no checks, no winning captures and no loose pieces. The position is calm and about plans, not tricks.
plans: Castle long next — O-O-O puts the rook on the d-file in one move.
Keep the pin while it annoys; only take on f6 for a concrete reason.
Centralise the rooks (Rd1/Rhe1) and probe the d6-pawn.
13.hook: Bg5 then O-O-O: pin first, castle into the d-file second.
13.mistake: Don't cash the pin with Bxf6 without a concrete reason — the pin is worth more than the bishop pair here.

15.why: 8.O-O-O castles, tucking the king to safety and connecting the rooks. Castling long completes the plan: the king reaches safety while the rook lands on d1 in the same move, staring straight down the d-file at Black's position.
15.logic: The payoff move of the whole setup: king safe, rook active on d1 the very same tempo — no other castling gains this much time.
15.prevents: Removes the king from the centre before the d-file pressure begins — the same file the rook now owns.
15.tactics: No immediate forcing tactics: no checks, no winning captures and no loose pieces. The position is calm and about plans, not tricks.
plans: Pile on the d-file: Rd1, sometimes doubling, against d6/d7.
Your kingside pawns are free to advance — h3/g4 gains space without risking your king.
Trade into favourable endgames; these positions convert themselves slowly.
15.hook: Castling long = king safe + rook on d1, same tempo.
15.mistake: Castling long means pawn storms can matter — glance at Black's ...b5-b4 potential before committing. In these lines White's centre and activity outrun it, but always look once.

35.summary: Material favours White by 9 point(s). Central pawns: White pawn on e4, Black pawn on e5. Development: White has 1 minor piece(s) out, Black 1. Loose/attacked pieces — White: queen on d8; Black: pawn on c7. It is Black's move.
35.why: 5.Qxd8+ the queen captures the queen on d8, with check. This queen trade is a *gain*, not a concession: Black must recapture awkwardly — ...Kxd8 loses castling forever, ...Nxd8 buries the knight — and White keeps a risk-free endgame pull.
35.logic: A signature structure of this repertoire: against ...d6 systems, trade queens on d8 and grind — Black's misplaced king and the weak e5-pawn outlast the symmetry.
35.prevents: Denies Black a middlegame: without queens, the counter-attacking chances Black's setup relied on evaporate, while the structural problems remain.
35.tactics: Tempting captures for Black: Kxd8, Nxd8. Loose White pieces: queen on d8 (undefended). Loose Black pieces: pawn on c7 (undefended).
plans: Exploit the stuck king: Bc4 eyes f7, rooks come to d1 fast.
Win or fix the e5-pawn (Nxe5 tricks when the defenders are gone).
Trade pieces, not pawns — every exchange magnifies the endgame edge.
35.hook: Qxd8+ ruins their day: their king gets stuck, yours doesn't.
35.mistake: Don't assume queen trades are drawish — this one wins time and castling rights. But count the captures on e5 carefully; the pawn-win tactic only works in the right order.

59.why: 5.Qxd4 the queen captures the pawn on d4. Normally the queen shouldn't come out early — but the c6-knight that would harass her has just been traded. She sits on d4 like a monarch: central, safe, and controlling both wings. This is the repertoire's core idea.
59.logic: The repertoire's pet line. After the knight trade the classic punishment for an early queen (a tempo-gaining ...Nc6) no longer exists, so White gets a dream setup for free: queen on d4, Nc3, Bg5 and O-O-O.
59.prevents: The immediate harassers are gone — that's the point. (...c5 or ...Bc5 can still nudge the queen later, which is exactly why the calm Qd1 retreat is part of the repertoire.)
plans: Develop Nc3 and Bg5 around the queen — she's a strongpoint, not a target.
Castle long: the rook lands on d1 with tempo against d6/d7.
If ...c5 arrives, retreat Qd1 calmly — the lead in development remains.
59.hook: Qxd4 is safe here — the harasser (that c6-knight) is gone.
59.mistake: Two opposite errors: avoiding 5.Qxd4 because 'the queen shouldn't come out early' (the rule's reason — tempo-gaining attacks — is absent here), and leaving her centralised once ...c5/...Bc5 arrive with real tempo. Centralise free, retreat taxed.

99.why: 5.Qxd8+ the queen captures the queen on d8, with check. This queen trade is a *gain*, not a concession: Black must recapture awkwardly — ...Kxd8 loses castling forever, ...Nxd8 buries the knight — and White keeps a risk-free endgame pull.
99.logic: A signature structure of this repertoire: against ...d6 systems, trade queens on d8 and grind — Black's misplaced king and the weak e5-pawn outlast the symmetry.
99.prevents: Denies Black a middlegame: without queens, the counter-attacking chances Black's setup relied on evaporate, while the structural problems remain.
99.tactics: Tempting captures for Black: Kxd8, Nxd8. Loose White pieces: queen on d8 (undefended). Loose Black pieces: pawn on c7 (undefended).

99.hook: Qxd8+ ruins their day: their king gets stuck, yours doesn't.
99.mistake: Don't assume queen trades are drawish — this one wins time and castling rights. But count the captures on e5 carefully; the pawn-win tactic only works in the right order.

107.logic: A signature structure of this repertoire: against ...d6 systems, trade queens on d8 and grind — Black's misplaced king and the weak e5-pawn outlast the symmetry.
107.prevents: Denies Black a middlegame: without queens, the counter-attacking chances Black's setup relied on evaporate, while the structural problems remain.
107.tactics: Tempting captures for Black: Kxd8. Loose White pieces: queen on d8 (undefended). Loose Black pieces: pawn on c7 (undefended).

107.mistake: Don't assume queen trades are drawish — this one wins time and castling rights. But count the captures on e5 carefully; the pawn-win tactic only works in the right order.

188.mistake: Don't prepare d4 slowly with c3 here (that's a different opening) — the strike works *because* it comes before Black is developed.

219.summary: Material is level. Central pawns: White pawn on e4. Development: White has 0 minor piece(s) out, Black 0. Kings — White is still in the centre (can still castle); Black is still in the centre (can still castle). Pawn structure is still healthy and symmetrical-ish. It is Black's move.
219.why: 5.Qxd4 the queen captures the knight on d4. Normally the queen shouldn't come out early — but the c6-knight that would harass her has just been traded. She sits on d4 like a monarch: central, safe, and controlling both wings. This is the repertoire's core idea.
219.logic: The repertoire's pet line. After the knight trade the classic punishment for an early queen (a tempo-gaining ...Nc6) no longer exists, so White gets a dream setup for free: queen on d4, Nc3, Bg5 and O-O-O.
219.prevents: The immediate harassers are gone — that's the point. (...c5 or ...Bc5 can still nudge the queen later, which is exactly why the calm Qd1 retreat is part of the repertoire.)
plans: Develop Nc3 and Bg5 around the queen — she's a strongpoint, not a target.
Castle long: the rook lands on d1 with tempo against d6/d7.
If ...c5 arrives, retreat Qd1 calmly — the lead in development remains.
219.hook: Qxd4 is safe here — the harasser (that c6-knight) is gone.
219.mistake: Two opposite errors: avoiding 5.Qxd4 because 'the queen shouldn't come out early' (the rule's reason — tempo-gaining attacks — is absent here), and leaving her centralised once ...c5/...Bc5 arrive with real tempo. Centralise free, retreat taxed.

223.summary: Material is level. Central pawns: White pawn on e4. Development: White has 2 minor piece(s) out, Black 1. Kings — White is still in the centre (can still castle); Black is still in the centre (can still castle). Pawn structure is still healthy and symmetrical-ish. It is Black's move.
223.why: 7.Bg5 develops/repositions the bishop to g5. Develops with pressure on the f6-knight. The pin kills ...Nxe4-style tricks and softens Black's kingside dark squares.
223.logic: The third piece of the Qxd4 machine: the pin ties Black's best defender to the board while White finishes the plan with O-O-O.
223.prevents: Freezes the f6-knight: no ...Nxe4 tricks, and any ...h6/...g5 attempt to break the pin weakens Black's own king.
223.tactics: No immediate forcing tactics: no checks, no winning captures and no loose pieces. The position is calm and about plans, not tricks.
plans: Castle long next — O-O-O puts the rook on the d-file in one move.
Keep the pin while it annoys; only take on f6 for a concrete reason.
Centralise the rooks (Rd1/Rhe1) and probe the d6-pawn.
223.hook: Bg5 then O-O-O: pin first, castle into the d-file second.
223.mistake: Don't cash the pin with Bxf6 without a concrete reason — the pin is worth more than the bishop pair here.

225.summary: Material is level. Central pawns: White pawn on e4. Development: White has 2 minor piece(s) out, Black 2. Kings — White is castled queenside; Black is still in the centre (can still castle). Pawn structure is still healthy and symmetrical-ish. It is Black's move.
225.why: 8.O-O-O castles, tucking the king to safety and connecting the rooks. Castling long completes the plan: the king reaches safety while the rook lands on d1 in the same move, staring straight down the d-file at Black's position.
225.logic: The payoff move of the whole setup: king safe, rook active on d1 the very same tempo — no other castling gains this much time.
225.prevents: Removes the king from the centre before the d-file pressure begins — the same file the rook now owns.
225.tactics: No immediate forcing tactics: no checks, no winning captures and no loose pieces. The position is calm and about plans, not tricks.
plans: Pile on the d-file: Rd1, sometimes doubling, against d6/d7.
Your kingside pawns are free to advance — h3/g4 gains space without risking your king.
Trade into favourable endgames; these positions convert themselves slowly.
225.hook: Castling long = king safe + rook on d1, same tempo.
225.mistake: Castling long means pawn storms can matter — glance at Black's ...b5-b4 potential before committing. In these lines White's centre and activity outrun it, but always look once.

245.logic: A signature structure of this repertoire: against ...d6 systems, trade queens on d8 and grind — Black's misplaced king and the weak e5-pawn outlast the symmetry.

269.why: 5.Qxd4 the queen captures the pawn on d4. Normally the queen shouldn't come out early — but the c6-knight that would harass her has just been traded. She sits on d4 like a monarch: central, safe, and controlling both wings. This is the repertoire's core idea.
269.logic: The repertoire's pet line. After the knight trade the classic punishment for an early queen (a tempo-gaining ...Nc6) no longer exists, so White gets a dream setup for free: queen on d4, Nc3, Bg5 and O-O-O.
269.prevents: The immediate harassers are gone — that's the point. (...c5 or ...Bc5 can still nudge the queen later, which is exactly why the calm Qd1 retreat is part of the repertoire.)
plans: Develop Nc3 and Bg5 around the queen — she's a strongpoint, not a target.
Castle long: the rook lands on d1 with tempo against d6/d7.
If ...c5 arrives, retreat Qd1 calmly — the lead in development remains.
269.hook: Qxd4 is safe here — the harasser (that c6-knight) is gone.
269.mistake: Two opposite errors: avoiding 5.Qxd4 because 'the queen shouldn't come out early' (the rule's reason — tempo-gaining attacks — is absent here), and leaving her centralised once ...c5/...Bc5 arrive with real tempo. Centralise free, retreat taxed.

315.summary: Material favours White by 5 point(s). Central pawns: White pawn on d4. Development: White has 2 minor piece(s) out, Black 1. Kings — White is still in the centre (can still castle); Black is in the centre, castling rights lost. Pawn structure: White has an isolated pawn on the d-file; Black has doubled pawns on the c-file; Black has an isolated pawn on the a, c, e-file. Loose/attacked pieces — White: pawn on g4, pawn on d4; Black: pawn on c6. It is Black's move.
315.why: 14.Qxc1+ the queen captures the bishop on c1, with check. Part of a forced refutation: a check, a capture or a direct threat that drags the Black king further up the board. Move order matters — follow the sequence exactly.
315.logic: The one truly forced line in the repertoire: the Fred refutation is a sequence of checks and threats worth learning move by move — it wins on the spot.
315.prevents: Each check denies the king a breathing move — one slow move and Black would consolidate, so the sequence prevents exactly that.

alts.idea: Castle the king into safety before continuing.
alts.why_not_preferred: Sensible, but the repertoire move addresses a more concrete need first.

315.tactics: Loose White pieces: pawn on g4 (attacked by a cheaper piece), pawn on d4 (undefended). Loose Black pieces: pawn on c6 (undefended).
plans: Finish development quickly — every piece toward the centre.
Keep or increase the central superiority; meet wing play with central play.
Choose the right castling: short in quiet lines, long in the Qxd4 setup.
315.hook: In the Fred: if it's not a check or a threat, it's not the move.
315.mistake: Inserting a 'safe' developing move in the middle of the forced sequence — that's the one way to let the king escape. Learn it as one chunk.

316.summary: Material favours White by 5 point(s). Central pawns: White pawn on d4. Development: White has 2 minor piece(s) out, Black 1. Kings — White is still in the centre (can still castle); Black is in the centre, castling rights lost. Pawn structure: White has an isolated pawn on the d-file; Black has doubled pawns on the c-file; Black has an isolated pawn on the a, c, e-file. Loose/attacked pieces — White: pawn on g4, pawn on d4; Black: pawn on c6. It is White's move.
316.why: 14...Kh4 develops/repositions the king to h4. Black flails for compensation that never existed — each try meets a forcing reply, and the king walks further into the storm.
316.logic: This move follows the repertoire's guiding ideas: open the centre early (d4!), develop with threats, centralise — even the queen, when she cannot be harassed — and steer for structures Black must defend precisely: queen-trade endgames, doubled pawns, kings stuck in the centre. Against Kh4, the repertoire continues 15.h3.
316.prevents: It keeps the initiative — the opponent now has to worry about the pawn on g4 rather than carrying out their own plan.
316.tactics: Checks for White: Ng6+, Nf3+, Qh6+, Qg5+. Tempting captures for White: Nxc6, Qxc6. Loose White pieces: pawn on g4 (attacked by a cheaper piece), pawn on d4 (undefended). Loose Black pieces: pawn on c6 (undefended).
plans: Finish development quickly — every piece toward the centre.
Keep or increase the central superiority; meet wing play with central play.
Choose the right castling: short in quiet lines, long in the Qxd4 setup.
316.hook: Remember 14...Kh4 as a natural building move in the Scotch Game setup.
316.mistake: Don't drift: keep developing and castle before starting an attack, and don't push pawns in front of your own king without a reason.

317.summary: Material favours White by 5 point(s). Central pawns: White pawn on d4. Development: White has 2 minor piece(s) out, Black 1. Kings — White is still in the centre (can still castle); Black is in the centre, castling rights lost. Pawn structure: White has an isolated pawn on the d-file; Black has doubled pawns on the c-file; Black has an isolated pawn on the a, c, e-file. Loose/attacked pieces — White: pawn on g4, pawn on d4, pawn on h3; Black: pawn on c6. It is Black's move.
317.why: 15.h3 advances a pawn to h3. A building move inside the refutation: it keeps the extra material, brings another piece toward the attack, and tightens the net before the next blow.
317.logic: Even inside a king hunt there are quiet moves: keep the material, add attackers, take squares from the king — then resume the checks.
317.prevents: It keeps the initiative — the opponent now has to worry about the pawn on c6 rather than carrying out their own plan.

alts.idea: Castle the king into safety before continuing.
alts.why_not_preferred: Sensible, but the repertoire move addresses a more concrete need first.

317.tactics: Tempting captures for Black: Qxd4. Loose White pieces: pawn on g4 (attacked by a cheaper piece), pawn on d4 (undefended), pawn on h3 (attacked by a cheaper piece). Loose Black pieces: pawn on c6 (undefended).
plans: Finish development quickly — every piece toward the centre.
Keep or increase the central superiority; meet wing play with central play.
Choose the right castling: short in quiet lines, long in the Qxd4 setup.
317.hook: Between the checks: add a piece, tighten the net.
317.mistake: Don't drift: keep developing and castle before starting an attack, and don't push pawns in front of your own king without a reason.
